# 409A Valuation: Triggers, Validity Window, and Timeline (2026)

## Summary
A 409A valuation is legally required before every stock option grant and must be renewed every 12 months from the valuation date or after any material event, whichever comes first. The 12-month clock resets from the valuation date, not the grant date. A signed term sheet for new financing can invalidate a current 409A by triggering a "material event" - this is the most commonly misunderstood rule. Without a valid 409A, employees face 20% federal penalty tax plus ordinary income tax and interest on their option spread under IRC Section 409A. Five categories of material events: financing events (priced rounds, down rounds, SAFE conversions), operational performance changes, strategic/structural changes, market/industry shifts, and M&A/liquidity events.

## Key quotations / statistics
- "A 409A valuation remains valid for 12 months from the valuation date, assuming no material events occur"
- "For example, a valuation dated January 15, 2026 is valid through January 14, 2027"
- "Best practice is to start the renewal process 2-3 weeks before expiration to avoid gaps"
- "The most common material event is raising a new financing round"
- "Even a signed term sheet for financing can invalidate a current 409A, rendering it 'stale'"
- "You must obtain a new 409A valuation at least every 12 months or after any material event—whichever comes first—before granting any additional stock options"
- "Using an expired valuation can expose employees to 20% federal penalty taxes plus interest"

## Annotations for stinger-forge
- This is the primary source for `guides/04-409a-valuations.md` trigger-and-validity section.
- The "signed term sheet invalidates 409A" rule is the single most dangerous gotcha for founders - it must be called out prominently with a warning block in the guide.
- The "start renewal 2-3 weeks before expiration" best practice is a concrete operational tip to include in the `templates/option-grant-checklist.md`.
- The five material event categories provide the structure for the trigger checklist in the 409A guide.
- IRS penalty regime (20% + ordinary income + interest) is the enforcement mechanism; always cite this when explaining why the 409A requirement is non-negotiable.
